title: 9.1 and 9.2 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lessons 9.1 and 9.2!

assessment_items.question_content:
>If globalization is to succeed, it must succeed for the poor and rich alike. It must deliver rights no less than riches. It must provide social justice and equity no less than economic prosperity and enhanced communication.

>— Kofi Annan, Secretary General of the United Nations (1997-2006)


**Which of the following statements best reflects Kofi Annan’s perspective on globalization?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: For globalization to succeed, it must help all people thrive by addressing poverty and inequality.
Globalization is already a success because wealth and communication advances have been shared equally.
Globalization has failed entirely in both economic growth and communication.
Globalization should focus solely on economic growth, not social issues like poverty and rights.

assessment_items.question_content:
>If globalization is to succeed, it must succeed for poor and rich alike. It must deliver rights no less than riches. It must provide social justice and equity no less than economic prosperity and enhanced communication.

>—Kofi Annan, Secretary-General of the United Nations (1997–2006)

**Which 20th-century event best supports Kofi Annan’s view that globalization should advance social justice and human rights?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The world adopted the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) in 1948.
The world responded collectively to the COVID-19 pandemic.
The invention of the World Wide Web in 1989 increased global communication.
The signing of the Paris Agreement encouraged global action on climate change.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is a way to reduce the negative effects of globalization while still benefiting from it?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Countries can encourage fair labor standards across global supply chains.
Countries can restrict all international trade to protect local economies.
Countries can avoid using foreign technology in domestic industries.
Countries can isolate their economies from the global financial system.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following factors has contributed most to the growth of global trade in recent decades?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Advancements in transportation and communication have increased global trade.
Higher taxes on imported goods around the world have increased global trade.
Decreased interest in foreign markets has encouraged global trade.
A reduction in natural resources has strengthened global trade.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What does the global journey of a T-shirt—from cotton grown in the U.S., to fabric production in China, to final assembly in another country—reveal about globalization?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The production of everyday items often depends on several countries working together.
Globalization makes production slower and more costly for most companies.
Most countries only manufacture goods for their own national markets.
Globalization has greatly reduced the income gap between wealthy and poor nations.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How does the production and distribution of a simple T-shirt demonstrate globalization?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: T-shirts reflect the complexity of global supply chains and the interconnectedness of economies.
T-shirt designs and trends often cross borders, promoting cultural exchange.
T-shirts sold in second-hand stores show how they continue contributing to the global economy.
Locally sourcing and producing T-shirts supports sustainable practices and local artisans.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Although NAFTA aimed to boost U.S. exports to Mexico and create jobs, what was its actual impact on the American workforce?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Thousands of jobs were lost as U.S. factories relocated to Mexico.
It successfully created an estimated 700,000 manufacturing jobs in the United States.
NAFTA strengthened unions and improved protections for factory workers.
It forced thousands of American workers to migrate to Mexico in search of work.

assessment_items.question_content:
The North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA) was promised to bring economic benefits throughout North America, including Mexico. 


**What impact did NAFTA have on Mexican society?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: NAFTA brought job growth in manufacturing but displaced many small farmers.
NAFTA led to widespread industrial growth in all sectors, including agriculture.
NAFTA eliminated poverty across rural regions of Mexico.
NAFTA caused Mexico to stop trading with countries outside North America.

assessment_items.question_content:
>Many Americans are still worried that this agreement will move jobs south of the border because they've seen jobs move south of the border and because they know that there are still great differences in the wage rates. There have been 19 serious economic studies of NAFTA by liberals and conservatives alike; 18 of them have concluded that there will be no job loss.

>—U.S. President William Clinton addressing American concerns about NAFTA

**Which fact about NAFTA most directly *contradicts* President Clinton’s claim?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: An estimated 700,000 manufacturing jobs were lost following the passage of NAFTA.
Thousands of jobs were created in export-based industries as a result of NAFTA.
U.S. agriculture exports increased as a result of open markets with Mexico and Canada.
NAFTA contributed to an economic surplus for the United States.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How do U.S. agricultural subsidies on crops like cotton and corn affect the global market?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They lower global prices, making it harder for farmers in other countries to compete.
They raise global food prices, helping farmers in developing countries earn more.
They encourage more farmers worldwide to grow these crops, increasing global supply.
The United States subsidizes farmers around the world to level the playing field.

assessment_items.question_content:
Demonstrations were held in 1999 to prevent the World Trade Organization (WTO) from meeting to negotiate the lowering of global trade barriers.

**What were some of the main arguments made in opposition to this kind of international policy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Critics argued that such policies created a “race to the bottom,” with corporations prioritizing profits over people.
Some believed these trade agreements favored other countries more than the United States.
Protesters opposed the WTO’s mission to promote peace through trade and called for U.S. isolationism.
Opponents objected to the WTO’s practice of requiring countries to cut social spending in exchange for aid.

assessment_items.question_content:
Globalization has generally had a positive impact on the world’s financial markets. However, the 2008 global recession exposed some of its vulnerabilities.

**What did the 2008 recession reveal about the relationship between globalization and the economy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Financial crises in one country can quickly spread to economies around the world.
The rise in fossil fuel use for global trade has increased pollution levels.
Global markets operate independently and are unaffected by national policies.
The recession showed how quickly diseases can spread in a globalized world.
